misconceptionSummary: Students think multiples are finite — they stop at some point rather than continuing forever.

visuals.ruleText: Multiples are what you land on when skip-counting in steps of n
visuals.subText: They form an infinite sequence with equal gaps of n

steps.narration: Imagine counting along a number line in equal jumps. If each jump is six units, you land on six, twelve, eighteen, twenty-four — these are the multiples of six. The jumps never stop, so neither does the list of multiples.
On a number line from zero to thirty-six, the multiples of six are evenly spaced at every sixth position. The pattern continues beyond thirty-six without end — every sixth number is a multiple.

lines.text: Multiple of n ↔ divisible by n

steps.narration: A number is a multiple of six if and only if it is exactly divisible by six. Twenty-four divides by six perfectly — it is a multiple. Twenty-five leaves a remainder — it is not.

visuals.label: Equal gaps of 6 — divisible by 6 with no remainder

steps.narration: Six, twelve, eighteen — an infinite sequence of equal steps. Every multiple is exactly divisible by the original number, and the sequence has no end.

misconceptionStrip.text: Common mistake: thinking multiples stop at a certain point (e.g. stopping at 60). Multiples are infinite — any number in the times table of n, no matter how large, is a multiple of n.
misconceptionStrip.audioNarration: Multiples never stop. You can always multiply n by a larger whole number to get the next multiple. The list is infinite.
